---
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    theme: cosmo
  docx: default
  pdf:
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
\noindent\rule{\textwidth}{1pt}
:::

**To:** MEE\@OIT Research Group, Oregon Institute of Technology\
**From:** Jesse Kinder\
**Subject:** Reproducible Analysis with TOML Configuration Files\
**Date:** 2026-04-22

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
\noindent\rule{\textwidth}{1pt}
:::

---

## Foreword

The MEE data analysis pipeline requires setting many parameters carefully and
consistently across multiple runs.
The goal of this work is to make the analysis reproducible by storing all
parameters in human-readable configuration files that can be shared,
version-controlled, and loaded directly into the GUI.

---

## Summary

The MEE pipeline involves three analysis stages — stacking images and finding
star centroids (Tab 1), computing the optical distortion (Tab 2), and fitting
the deflection data (Tab 3).  Each stage depends on a dozen or more parameters:
detection thresholds, distortion polynomial order, observing conditions,
limiting magnitudes, and more.  Currently, these are adjusted by hand each time
the GUI is opened.  There is no record of which values were used for a given
run, making it difficult to reproduce a result or to compare two runs that
differ only in one parameter.

The solution introduced here is a set of TOML configuration files — one per
pipeline stage.  TOML is a simple, human-readable format that supports
comments, so each parameter can be documented alongside its value.  A "Load
Config" button was added to the top of each GUI tab.  Clicking it reads a
configuration file and populates all fields automatically, replacing manual
entry.  In addition, when the Run button is clicked, the exact settings used
for that run are automatically written to the output folder as a configuration
file.  This means every output folder is self-documenting: the configuration
that produced it is stored alongside the results, even if fields were adjusted
after loading.

A complete, successful analysis of the Station 1 sample data was carried out
using only TOML configuration files as input.  All five pipeline stages ran
without manually adjusting a single field in the GUI.

---

## Discussion

### Configuration Files for Station 1

The Station 1 pipeline requires five configuration files, one per analysis
step:

| File | Tab | Purpose |
|------|-----|---------|
| `find-stars-calibration.toml` | Tab 1 | Stack zenith/calibration images, find centroids |
| `find-stars-eclipse.toml` | Tab 1 | Stack eclipse images, find centroids |
| `compute-distortion-zenith.toml` | Tab 2 | Fit full distortion polynomial from zenith data |
| `compute-distortion-eclipse.toml` | Tab 2 | Re-fit plate scale only, fixing high-order terms |
| `fit-data.toml` | Tab 3 | Measure stellar deflections, fit Einstein coefficient |

The first two are loaded in separate Tab 1 runs; the next two in separate Tab 2
runs; the last in Tab 3.  Each file contains all parameters needed for that
stage, with comments explaining what each parameter controls.

### What a Configuration File Looks Like

Below is a short excerpt from the zenith Tab 2 configuration file.

```toml
# Station 1 — compute distortion (zenith field)
# Observation: 2024-04-07, Durango, Mexico

[input]
input_folder = "/path/to/output/station1-calibration"

[fit]
distortionOrder          = "quintic"   # ZWO ASI6200MM requires quintic
distortion_fit_tol       = 2.0         # arcseconds; loosen slightly for wide-field
rough_match_threshhold   = 100.0       # arcsec; wider window for first pass
distortion_fixed_coefficients = "None" # free fit — all coefficients float

[observatory]
observation_date     = "2024-04-07"
observation_lat      = "23 50 58.3 N"
observation_long     = "105 16 22.1 W"
observation_height   = 2400.0          # meters
observation_wavelength = 0.625         # microns (red filter)
```

Every parameter has a comment explaining what it does and, where relevant, why
a particular value was chosen.  New users can read the file and understand
the analysis without prior knowledge of the code.

### Loading a Configuration in the GUI

Loading a configuration is a two-step process.  At the top of each tab there
is a file field with two buttons: **Browse** and **Load Config**.

1. Click **Browse** to open a file dialog and select a `.toml` file.  The path
   appears in the field to the left.
2. Click **Load Config** to import the settings.  All GUI fields on that tab
   are updated immediately, and a small popup confirms the file was loaded.

The Browse/Load separation is intentional: selecting a file does not change
anything until Load is explicitly clicked.

### The Output Configuration File

When the Run button is clicked, the settings currently in the GUI are written
to a file called `config_tab1.toml`, `config_tab2.toml`, or `config_tab3.toml`
in the output folder, *before* processing begins.  This is distinct from the
input configuration: it records what was actually used, including any manual
adjustments made after loading.

This makes it possible to recover the exact analysis parameters for any output
folder, which is essential for comparing runs and for publishing reproducible
results.

### A Known Limitation: Timestamped Output Files

The current GUI names its output files with a timestamp embedded in the
filename — for example, `centroid_data20260422143012.zip`.  Because the
timestamp is not known when the configuration file is written, it is not
possible to hard-code the name of a later tab's input file in the
configuration.

The current workaround: when a Tab 2 or Tab 3 configuration is loaded and no
explicit input file is specified, the GUI searches the output directory for
the most recently modified file matching the expected pattern
(`centroid_data*.zip` for Tab 2; `distortion_data*.zip` for Tab 3) and
populates the file field automatically.  This is reliable for a single,
sequential pipeline run.  It would fail if multiple output files from
different runs were present in the same folder — a reason to keep output
folders organized.

A future improvement will replace the timestamped ZIP files with
descriptively named outputs (`{run}_centroids.csv`, `{run}_distortion.toml`,
etc.) that can be specified in configuration files directly.

### GUI Improvements

Two usability improvements were made at the same time.  The font size was
increased from the system default to 14 pt, making the labels and fields
significantly more legible.  A vertical scrollbar was added to each tab so
that all fields remain accessible regardless of window height or screen size.
The window is also now resizable.

### Future Work

The immediate value of this workflow is eliminating manual parameter entry and
creating an audit trail.  The deeper value is that it enables systematic,
reproducible studies of how the analysis choices affect the final result.
Some questions that are now straightforward to investigate:

- **Choice of calibration field.**  Does the particular star field used for
  the zenith calibration affect the distortion coefficients, and if so, by
  how much?  Run the analysis with two or more different calibration sets and
  compare.

- **Number of eclipse images.**  How does precision improve as more eclipse
  frames are included?  Run the analysis on subsets of 10, 20, 50, 100, ...
  images and plot precision as a function of sample size.

- **Parameter sensitivity.**  Which analysis parameters have the most
  significant effect on the measured deflection?  A grid of runs varying one
  parameter at a time would reveal the most important knobs to control.

These studies are easy to describe but require many pipeline runs.  The next
development step is a set of command-line scripts that can run each stage
without opening the GUI, making batch processing and automated parameter
sweeps practical.
